title: Reliance Oil Expands Its Network to Northern Ghana
author: Kwame Asante-Mensah
excerpt: Reliance Oil Limited has officially commissioned three new filling stations in the Northern, Upper East, and Brong-Ahafo regions, extending quality fuel access to underserved communities. This expansion marks a major milestone in the company's five-year growth strategy.
content:
Reliance Oil Limited has taken a decisive step in fulfilling its vision of nationwide petroleum access with the commissioning of three state-of-the-art filling stations across Ghana's northern corridor. The new outlets, located in Tamale, Bolgatanga, and Techiman, bring the company's total station count to 32, making Reliance Oil one of the fastest-growing downstream petroleum companies in the country.

The expansion project, which began in early 2024, involved significant investment in infrastructure, safety systems, and staff training. Each new station features modern multi-product dispensers, underground storage tanks that meet international environmental standards, and dedicated LPG refilling facilities. The stations have also been designed to accommodate future service additions such as electric vehicle charging points.

Chief Executive Officer Kwame Asante-Mensah stated that the company's northern expansion was driven by both commercial opportunity and a genuine commitment to equitable access to energy. 'We believe every Ghanaian, regardless of location, deserves access to safe, high-quality petroleum products at fair prices,' he said during the commissioning ceremony in Tamale. 'These three stations are not just business investments — they are community investments.'

Reliance Oil has also announced plans to recruit over 90 local staff across the three new locations, contributing meaningfully to employment in communities where job opportunities in the formal sector remain limited. The company is simultaneously partnering with regional transport unions to roll out its fleet management smart card programme, which provides corporate fuel discounts and real-time consumption tracking to registered fleet operators.

title: Reliance Oil Awards Scholarships to 50 Students Across Ghana
author: Efua Mensah-Bonney
excerpt: As part of its Corporate Social Responsibility programme, Reliance Oil Limited has awarded full scholarships to 50 brilliant but financially challenged students from communities near its filling stations. The initiative underscores the company's belief that education is the most sustainable investment a business can make.
content:
Reliance Oil Limited's 2024 Scholarship Programme has officially been launched, with 50 students from across eight regions of Ghana receiving full academic scholarships covering tuition, accommodation, and study materials. Recipients were selected based on academic excellence and demonstrated financial need, with particular consideration given to students from communities where Reliance Oil operates filling stations.

The scholarship programme is part of Reliance Oil's broader CSR framework, which focuses on education, environmental sustainability, and community health. This year's recipients include 22 female students, reflecting the company's deliberate effort to promote gender equity in education. Scholarship recipients are enrolled in programmes spanning engineering, business administration, environmental science, and medicine at universities across Ghana.

HR Manager Efua Mensah-Bonney, who oversees the company's social investment portfolio, described the programme as one of the company's most impactful initiatives. 'We are not just selling fuel — we are investing in the human capital that will drive Ghana's future economy,' she said. 'These 50 young people represent the potential of our communities, and we are proud to support their journeys.'

Reliance Oil has committed to expanding the scholarship programme in 2025, with plans to increase the number of beneficiaries to 75 and introduce a mentorship component that will connect scholarship recipients with senior professionals within the company and its partner organisations.

title: Ghana's Petroleum Downstream Sector: Outlook for 2025
author: Daniel Antwi-Boasiako
excerpt: The downstream petroleum sector in Ghana is poised for significant transformation in 2025, driven by regulatory reform, increasing demand, and the entry of new market participants. Industry analysts project steady growth in fuel consumption as Ghana's economy continues its post-pandemic recovery.
content:
Ghana's downstream petroleum sector enters 2025 with cautious optimism, underpinned by a recovering macroeconomy, a more stable currency, and a regulatory environment that has become increasingly supportive of private sector participation. The National Petroleum Authority (NPA) has signalled its intention to introduce a revised pricing framework designed to improve transparency and reduce fuel queue incidents at major urban filling stations.

Consumption of refined petroleum products in Ghana is projected to increase by approximately 8% in 2025, according to estimates from the Ghana Energy Commission. The growth will be driven by rising motorisation rates, infrastructure expansion, and increased industrial activity in the mining and construction sectors. Diesel demand in particular is expected to surge as several large infrastructure projects awarded under the government's road rehabilitation programme move into their construction phases.

The LPG segment continues to attract significant attention as the government pushes forward with its Cylinder Recirculation Model, which aims to increase LPG penetration in rural and peri-urban households. Companies that have invested in LPG infrastructure — including Reliance Oil — are well-positioned to benefit from this growing market. Analysts note that the shift away from charcoal and firewood as primary cooking fuels represents a generational opportunity for downstream petroleum companies.

The sector also faces headwinds, particularly around the continued depreciation of the cedi against major currencies, which keeps import costs elevated. Companies with diversified revenue streams and strong operational efficiency will be best placed to navigate these pressures. For Reliance Oil, investments in technology, fleet management services, and a growing convenience retail portfolio position it well for sustained performance in the year ahead.

title: Reliance Oil Completes Nationwide Fire Safety Training for Station Staff
author: Emmanuel Adjei-Korsah
excerpt: Reliance Oil Limited has successfully completed a mandatory fire safety training programme covering all staff at its 32 filling stations nationwide. The exercise, conducted in partnership with the Ghana National Fire Service, represents the company's most comprehensive safety initiative to date.
content:
Reliance Oil Limited has concluded a four-month nationwide fire safety training programme, ensuring that every frontline employee at its filling station network is equipped with the knowledge and skills to prevent, detect, and respond to fire emergencies. The training, delivered in collaboration with the Ghana National Fire Service, covered fire classification, extinguisher operation, evacuation procedures, and first aid response.

Over 640 staff members participated in the training across 11 cities and towns, with sessions conducted at actual station sites to ensure that employees practised procedures in familiar, real-world environments. Station managers additionally received advanced training in emergency response coordination and incident reporting, bringing Reliance Oil's internal safety protocols in line with international downstream petroleum standards.

Operations Manager Emmanuel Adjei-Korsah noted that the training represented a significant upgrade in the company's safety culture. 'A safe station is a productive station,' he said. 'Our customers and employees deserve to operate in an environment where every risk has been anticipated and every staff member knows exactly what to do in an emergency.' He added that all stations have been re-equipped with new fire extinguishers, emergency cut-off switches, and updated safety signage following the training.

Reliance Oil has announced that fire safety training will henceforth be conducted on a biannual basis, with refresher modules incorporated into the onboarding process for new hires. The company is also piloting the installation of automatic fire suppression systems at its highest-traffic stations in Accra, Kumasi, and Takoradi, with plans to extend coverage to the full network by 2026.

title: Reliance Oil Launches Smart Fleet Card for Corporate Clients
author: Mavis Quartey-Papafio
excerpt: Reliance Oil has officially launched its Reliance FleetCard, a smart fuel management solution designed to help corporate clients track, control, and reduce their fuel expenditure across the company's nationwide station network. The product has already attracted over 40 corporate clients since its soft launch in November 2024.
content:
Reliance Oil Limited has formally introduced its Reliance FleetCard to the Ghanaian corporate market, offering businesses a digital solution to manage fuel consumption across their vehicle fleets. The card system provides real-time transaction data, spending limits, and monthly analytical reports, giving fleet managers the tools to eliminate fuel theft, reduce waste, and negotiate better pricing based on consumption volume.

The FleetCard is accepted at all 32 Reliance Oil stations across Ghana, and the company is in advanced discussions with partner fuel retailers to extend acceptance to additional sites by mid-2025. Corporate clients who sign up receive dedicated account managers, priority service lanes at participating stations, and access to Reliance Oil's online portal where they can manage card allocations, set per-trip fuel limits, and download audit-ready expense reports.

Marketing Manager Mavis Quartey-Papafio described the FleetCard as a product born out of direct client feedback. 'We listened to our corporate customers and heard a consistent pain point: fuel management was opaque, time-consuming, and vulnerable to abuse,' she said. 'The FleetCard addresses all of those issues in one integrated platform.' Early adopters from the logistics, construction, and agriculture sectors have reported average fuel cost reductions of between 12% and 18% within the first quarter of use.

Reliance Oil plans to integrate telematics data from partner GPS providers into the FleetCard platform by the third quarter of 2025, enabling clients to correlate fuel usage with route data for even deeper operational insights. The company has also signalled intentions to introduce a loyalty rewards component for high-volume clients, further strengthening the value proposition of the product.

title: Reliance Oil Plants 5,000 Trees to Mark World Environment Day
author: Abena Osei-Bonsu
excerpt: On World Environment Day, Reliance Oil Limited mobilised staff, partners, and community volunteers across 10 regions to plant 5,000 trees as part of its Green Ghana initiative. The effort reflects the company's commitment to offsetting its environmental footprint and contributing to national reforestation goals.
content:
Reliance Oil Limited joined hundreds of organisations across Ghana in marking World Environment Day with a large-scale tree planting exercise that saw 5,000 seedlings planted across 10 regions of the country. The exercise, which involved over 300 staff volunteers alongside community members and local school children, took place simultaneously at filling stations and community green spaces in Accra, Kumasi, Takoradi, Ho, Tamale, Cape Coast, Koforidua, Sunyani, Bolgatanga, and Tema.

The initiative is part of Reliance Oil's broader Green Commitment framework, which also includes reduced-emission fuel options, waste management programmes at its station network, and phased investment in solar-powered station lighting. The company has pledged to plant a minimum of 5,000 trees annually for the next five years, contributing to the Forestry Commission's national target of restoring degraded forest cover across Ghana.

Chief Operating Officer Abena Osei-Bonsu led the planting ceremony at the Accra Central station alongside schoolchildren from a nearby primary school. 'We are in the business of energy, and we recognise that our activities have an environmental footprint,' she said. 'This exercise is not about optics — it is about accountability, and about leaving Ghana greener than we found it.' She invited all Ghanaians to see Reliance Oil as a partner, not just a fuel provider, in the national sustainability agenda.

Reliance Oil has also partnered with the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to conduct annual environmental audits of its station network, ensuring that underground storage tanks, drainage systems, and waste disposal practices meet regulatory standards. The company's investment in low-sulphur fuel options and bio diesel further demonstrates its intention to align commercial growth with environmental responsibility.

title: LPG in Ghana: Opportunities and Challenges for Downstream Players
author: Daniel Antwi-Boasiako
excerpt: As Ghana's government accelerates its Cylinder Recirculation Model, LPG is emerging as a critical growth segment for downstream petroleum companies. This article examines the opportunities and structural challenges that define the LPG market landscape.
content:
Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) represents one of the most dynamic growth opportunities in Ghana's downstream energy sector, driven by government policy, rising consumer incomes, and growing awareness of the health hazards associated with solid fuel combustion. The National Petroleum Authority estimates that LPG penetration currently sits at approximately 35% of Ghanaian households, with significant headroom for growth particularly in rural and peri-urban communities.

The government's Cylinder Recirculation Model, which standardises cylinder sizes and enables safe, regulated refilling across a certified network of outlets, has provided a structured framework for downstream companies to invest in LPG infrastructure with greater confidence. Reliance Oil has positioned itself as an early adopter of the model, with LPG facilities available at 14 of its 32 stations and plans to extend coverage to 22 stations by the end of 2025.

Despite the opportunity, the LPG sector is not without challenges. Infrastructure costs remain high, particularly for the construction of safe, NPA-compliant LPG storage and dispensing facilities. Supply-side volatility, linked to Ghana's dependence on imported LPG and fluctuating international commodity prices, continues to create periodic shortages that erode consumer confidence. Companies operating in this space must invest in robust supply chain management and maintain strategic reserves to buffer against these disruptions.

For downstream players with the capital and operational capability to navigate these challenges, the LPG segment offers attractive margins and the kind of consumer loyalty that is harder to achieve in the commoditised petrol and diesel retail market. Reliance Oil's investment in trained LPG technicians, safety-first station design, and consumer education programmes positions it well to capture a growing share of this market as penetration rates continue to rise.

title: Reliance Oil Launches 'Drive Safe Ghana' Road Safety Campaign
author: Emmanuel Adjei-Korsah
excerpt: Reliance Oil Limited has partnered with the National Road Safety Authority to launch the 'Drive Safe Ghana' campaign, promoting responsible driving habits among Ghanaian motorists. The campaign includes free vehicle checks, safety materials distribution, and awareness activations at all Reliance Oil stations.
content:
Reliance Oil Limited has launched a nationwide road safety campaign in partnership with the National Road Safety Authority (NRSA), targeting the reduction of road traffic accidents in Ghana. Titled 'Drive Safe Ghana,' the campaign runs from October to December 2024 and encompasses free vehicle safety checks, tyre pressure inspections, brake fluid level assessments, and the distribution of road safety information packs at all 32 Reliance Oil stations.

The campaign was motivated by statistics showing that vehicle mechanical failure remains one of the leading contributory factors in road traffic accidents in Ghana. By leveraging its extensive station network as community touchpoints, Reliance Oil aims to make basic vehicle safety checks accessible to all motorists, including those who may not regularly visit formal vehicle inspection centres. Trained technicians are stationed at participating outlets during peak hours to conduct rapid safety assessments at no charge.

Operations Manager Emmanuel Adjei-Korsah emphasised the alignment between Reliance Oil's core business and road safety. 'Every vehicle that passes through our station is an opportunity to make Ghana's roads safer,' he said. 'Our staff are trained not just to dispense fuel but to be genuine service partners who look out for our customers' wellbeing.' He noted that during the campaign's first two weeks, technicians had identified potentially dangerous tyre or brake conditions in over 200 vehicles.

The 'Drive Safe Ghana' campaign also includes a social media component encouraging motorists to share their road safety commitments using a dedicated hashtag. Reliance Oil has partnered with several radio stations in Accra, Kumasi, and Tamale to broadcast road safety messages during morning and evening peak hours, maximising reach to daily commuters across the country's three largest cities.

title: Reliance Oil 2024 Annual Performance: Growth Across All Segments
author: Kofi Darko-Ampem
excerpt: Reliance Oil Limited closed the 2024 financial year with double-digit revenue growth, driven by network expansion, the successful launch of the FleetCard product, and strong LPG segment performance. The CFO outlines the key drivers and the company's financial outlook for 2025.
content:
Reliance Oil Limited concluded the 2024 financial year with strong results across all business segments, recording a 16% increase in total revenue compared to 2023. The performance was underpinned by the commissioning of three new filling stations in the second half of the year, robust consumer demand for petrol and diesel, and accelerating uptake of the company's LPG and lubricants product lines. The Fleet Management division, which launched commercially in late 2024, also made a positive early contribution to revenues.

The company's gross margin remained stable despite the challenging macroeconomic environment characterised by cedi depreciation and elevated import costs. Reliance Oil's procurement strategy, which includes forward purchasing agreements and diversified supplier relationships, helped insulate the business from the worst of the commodity price volatility that affected many peers in the sector. The company maintained a healthy cash position and successfully refinanced a portion of its long-term debt at improved terms during the second quarter.

Chief Financial Officer Kofi Darko-Ampem attributed the results to the discipline of the operational teams and the confidence of the company's financial partners. 'Our growth in 2024 was not accidental — it was the result of careful planning, disciplined execution, and a willingness to invest in the future when others were pulling back,' he said. He noted that the company had invested approximately GHS 45 million in capital expenditure during the year, primarily in new station construction and technology infrastructure.

Looking ahead to 2025, Reliance Oil has set targets for continued network expansion, with plans to commission four additional stations and extend FleetCard acceptance to over 200 vehicles. The company will also invest in digital infrastructure, including a customer-facing mobile application that will allow motorists to locate the nearest Reliance Oil station, check fuel prices, and manage loyalty rewards in real time. Management remains confident that Reliance Oil is well-positioned for another year of strong, sustainable growth.
